exemplar.title: The Effectiveness of Coffee Grounds in Preventing Soil Erosion and Enhancing Plant Growth

detailedExemplarData.title: The Effectiveness of Coffee Grounds in Preventing Soil Erosion and Enhancing Plant Growth

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows a logical IB IA structure with clear headings
Generally coherent flow from context through to conclusions
weaknesses: Table of contents and numbering overlap and are inconsistent
Figures are referenced out of sequence and tables overlap pages
Some prose is wordy and formatting lapses hinder readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of (2, 2, 2) place Communication firmly in the 2âmark band.  The student makes correct use of terminology and maintains overall coherence, but recurring structural and formatting issues limit the mark to 2.

strengths: Research question is coherent, focused, quantitative and time-bounded
Environmental context is detailed, with scale and causes of soil degradation
Clear links drawn between coffee grounds as amendment and soil-quality improvement
weaknesses: Minor ambiguity in phrasing (âhelp with the growthâ vs âaffect the growthâ)
Connection to erosion mechanics could be deeper
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The three strand scores (5, 5, 4) average into the upper band for Identifying the Context.  The studentâs question is precise and well framed, the environmental issue is thoroughly contextualized, and the link to the research has only minor omissions.  A mid-to-high band mark of 5 is therefore justified.

strengths: Procedure is clear and repeatable step by step
Sampling of 20 seeds demonstrates some consideration of data collection
Basic risks and ethical considerations are acknowledged
weaknesses: Only one pot per treatment and uncontrolled light limit data sufficiency
Sampling justification omits replication and randomization rationale
Risk assessment lacks severity/probability ratings and mitigation detail
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores (4, 3, 3) place Planning solidly in the 3â4 band.  The method is repeatable but limited in data robustness, sampling is described rather than fully justified, and risk/ethical issues are only outlined.  A score of 3 reflects these strengths and shortcomings.

strengths: Evaluation links the conclusion back to soil-quality improvement and fertiliser replacement
Method strengths and weaknesses are identified with corrective suggestions
Meaningful improvements (root length, individual trays) are proposed
weaknesses: Omission of erosion metrics or socio-economic context in the evaluation
Limitations (replication, statistics) are not fully explored
Further research plans remain general rather than specific
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands scored 3, indicating a consistent 3â4 band performance.  The student evaluates findings and method and proposes extensions, but does not fully explore limitations or broader implications, warranting a 3.

strengths: Data are organized into tables and processed into graphs with appropriate titles and units
Basic analyses (means, ranges, growth rates) correctly identify key trends
Conclusion references numerical results to support the inverted-U pattern
weaknesses: Tables and figures contain formatting errors and omissions (merged cells, missing units)
No error bars or statistical tests are included
Raw data tables have blank entries without explanation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With strand assessments of (3, 3, 4), the work fits the lower half of the 3â4 band for Results, Analysis and Conclusion.  While data presentation and interpretation are present, serious omissions (formatting, missing error analysis) limit the mark to 3.

strengths: Proposes a specific soil-to-coffee ratio as a potential application
Links the idea to nutrient enrichment and reduced chemical inputs
weaknesses: Ratio choice is not justified with reference to optimal 40 % finding
Evaluation omits social, economic and ecological considerations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although an application is stated, the justification is superficial and fails to connect the proposed ratio to the experimental data or address broader feasibility.  This limited fulfilment aligns more closely with band 1 (âstates one potential applicationâ) than band 2, justifying a score of 1.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific terms (germination, standard deviation, soil amendment) are used correctly, but prose is wordy and occasionally colloquial (âNext time, by picking an open spaceâ¦â).  This satisfies âeither terminology or concisionâ, not both, so band 2 is appropriate.

Part A links findings to soil-quality improvement and fertiliser replacement, noting possible nutrient-uptake interference.  The evaluation acknowledges potential long-term effects yet omits discussion of erosion metrics or wider socio-economic factors.  These omissions mean the evaluation is present but incomplete, aligning with the 3â4 band; a score of 3 is appropriate.

The conclusion explicitly references numerical outcomes (7.9 cm vs 5.6 cm) and interprets the inverted-U trend in relation to nutrient supply and possible acidity.  Assertions are supported by the presented averages, fulfilling âinterprets trends for a conclusion with some validityâ.  Minor over-generalisations and absence of significance testing prevent the highest band, so a high 3â4 band score of 4 is assigned.

Means, ranges and crude âgrowth ratesâ are calculated, and patterns (optimal at 40 % coffee) are identified.  Nonetheless, the standard-deviation formula is incorrect, no statistical test (e.g., ANOVA) is applied, and error propagation is absent.  Analysis is thus correct in parts but incomplete, fitting the 3â4 descriptor; score 3 reflects the noticeable gaps.

The student suggests individual trays, equal light exposure and measuring root length as improvements/extensions.  While meaningful, they do not fully address major design flaws (lack of replication, statistical treatment) nor outline specific further research plans.  Hence suggestions are âmeaningfulâ but not âsignificantâ, placing the work at band 3â4 with a score of 3.

A step-by-step procedure (18 numbered steps) details volumes of soil, coffee and water, placement, germination observations and measurement technique.  Another student could replicate the work, and the method would yield data relevant to the question.  Nevertheless, only one pot per treatment is used, light is uncontrolled and the watering regime contradicts itself (50 mL soil-mixing vs 10 mL spraying), so sufficiency is limited.  This aligns with ârepeatable â¦ but insufficient for collecting sufficient relevant dataââtop of the 3â4 band, score 4.

Links between the issue (soil degradation/erosion from chemical fertilisers) and the experiment (adding coffee grounds as an organic amendment) are described: coffee supplies N, P, K, improves structure and could replace synthetics.  However, the explanation of how measuring mung-bean height over 13 days will illuminate soil-erosion prevention is somewhat superficial, and the potential mechanism (reduced erosion) is not analysed in depth.  Connections are therefore described with some omissions, matching the 3â4 band; the stronger end (4) is selected.

The research question is clearly stated, quantitative and time-bounded: âTo what extent can coffee grounds â¦ after 13 days?â  It specifies both the independent (percentage of coffee grounds) and dependent (mean height of mung-bean seedlings) variables and indicates the measurement unit (cm).  The wording is coherent, focused and free from extraneous aims, so it best fits the 5â6 band.  Minor ambiguity about âhelp with the growthâ rather than âaffect the growthâ slightly detracts from ultimate polish, so a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

Strengths (nutrient enrichment, reduced chemical fertiliser) and weaknesses (transport cost, labour intensity, crop specificity) are mentioned, but not all relevant economic, social and ecological factors are weighed.  Therefore the solution is âevaluatedâ but only partially, matching the 2-mark band.

The report follows the IB ESS IA logical order with clear headings and a contents page, yet some tables overlap pages and numbering is inconsistent.  Organisation is present but not sustained flawlessly, corresponding to band 2 (structure but lapses).

Part B lists strengths (new seed packet, equal watering) and weaknesses (leakage, uneven light) with corresponding fixes.  Limitations such as single-pot replication or uncontrolled temperature are not explored.  Thus strengths, weaknesses and some limitations are described but not exhaustively discussed, satisfying the 3â4 band; a mid-score 3 is selected.

The student discusses Viet Namâs heavy pesticide/fertiliser use, food-waste rates and soil degradation, quoting tonnage figures and several sources.  They explain nutrient content of spent coffee and link this to soil quality and erosion.  The description goes beyond a brief outline by presenting scale, causes and consequences, so the work meets the descriptor of a ârelevant environmental issue that provides contextâ, meriting the 5â6 band.  Some statistics are loosely referenced, therefore a score of 5 (rather than 6) is awarded.

The student explains why 20 seeds were placed in each pot and why fresh, uncontaminated seeds were chosen, but does not address replication (only one pot per percentage) or randomisation.  Rationale for the 13-day duration is asserted rather than justified, and no discussion of sample size relative to statistical treatment appears.  Thus the strand is âdescribedâ rather than âjustifiedâ, placing it in the 3â4 band; a lower-band score of 3 is appropriate.

Basic biological safety (hand-washing), glassware injury avoidance and ethical disposal are listed.  The student also notes avoidance of data falsification.  However, no severity ratings, probability estimates or mitigation hierarchy is given, and ethical discussion is minimal (no biodiversity impacts, waste management detail, etc.).  This constitutes an outline rather than a comprehensive description, so band 3â4 fits; a score of 3 is awarded.

Most raw data are organised in tables and two processed graphs are provided.  Titles and units appear, yet several tables are incomplete, mis-labelled or formatted poorly (e.g., merged headings, missing units; figures numbered inconsistently).  Standard-deviation bars are mentioned but not shown.  The presentation is therefore appropriate but with omissions, matching the 3â4 band; a mid-score of 3 is given.

The application section proposes using a 3:2 soil-to-coffee ratio as an alternative fertiliser and briefly explains how this could reduce chemical inputs in Viet Nam.  Some justification is offered (nutrient enrichment, erosion reduction) but depth is limited and feasibility analysis is minimal.  This fits the descriptor âdescribes one potential application/solution with weak justificationââband 2, awarding 2 marks.

Arguments generally follow a logical flow from context to planning to results.  However, internal coherence suffers where methods contradict (10 mL vs 50 mL water) and where figures/tables are referenced incorrectly.  The work is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, aligning with band 2.

annotations.comment: The title succinctly conveys both aspects of the studyâsoil erosion prevention and plant growth enhancementâproviding an effective overview of the investigation.
The table of contents shows overlapping entries and inconsistent numbering; ensure clear, non-overlapping entries with correct page references.
âHelp with the growthâ is vague; revise to âaffect the growthâ or âinfluence the heightâ for precision in the research question.
Presentation of variables and justifications in a single paragraph makes distinctions unclear; separate into a clear table for concision.
Light and temperature are not listed under controls; add these to maintain consistency of growth conditions.
Sampling justification omits replication and randomization; include rationale for pot number and seed placement procedures to strengthen sampling design.
Apparatus table uses inconsistent units (liters vs. ml); standardize units (e.g., convert all volumes to mL) for clarity.
Risk assessment lacks probability and severity ratings; expand to include quantitative risk scoring and detailed mitigation steps.
Method contradicts watering volumes: step 13 uses 50 mL whereas step 17 sprays 10 mL; clarify consistent watering regime.
Step 18 is blank in the procedure; ensure all steps are fully detailed or remove the extraneous numbering.
Suggest integrating measurement of root length and biomass to deepen evaluation of coffee groundsâ impact on overall plant health.
Application proposes a 3:2 soilâcoffee ratio without linking to data; justify ratio choice referring to the optimal 40% result.
Solution evaluation lists cost concerns but omits discussion of scale and logistics; include quantitative costâbenefit analysis.
The environmental issue description effectively communicates scale but lacks deeper linkage to erosion mechanics; explain how coffee grounds mitigate runoff.
The null hypothesis should state no effect explicitly: âIncreased coffee grounds will not significantly change mung bean growth.â
Qualitative observations mention growth trends but do not link to soil erosion context; relate observations back to environmental issue.
Figures lack scale bars and detailed captions (e.g., magnification, time point); add these to improve clarity.
Raw data tables for days 1â3 contain only N/A entries; present complete data or explain missing germination on those days.
Table 2 has formatting errors and merged cells that obscure data; reformat header row and ensure each data cell is distinct.
Numerous blank cells in raw data tables (pages 10â14) hinder pattern recognition; denote zeros or N/A with explanatory footnotes.
The formula for standard deviation is incorrect; use $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \overline x)^2}{n-1}}$$ and show error bars in graphs.
Processed data table lacks units and decimal consistency; include âcm/dâ heading and maintain uniform decimal places.
Figures 3â5 show trends but omit error bars, which are essential for assessing variability; add standard-deviation bars.
Narrative refers to âfigure 5â before figure 3, disrupting coherence; order figures in the sequence they are mentioned.
Discussion Part A acknowledges soil quality but omits erosion measurement; evaluate how plant cover influences runoff and sediment retention.
The strength claims 100 seeds but method used 20 per pot; clarify how surplus seeds were handled to avoid confusion.
Works Cited entries show truncated URLs and missing access dates; ensure each citation is complete and follows a consistent style.
